The [Kingdom of Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium") is divided into three [regions](/wiki/Communities,_regions,_and_language_areas_of_Belgium "Communities, regions, and language areas of Belgium"). Two of these regions, [Flanders](/wiki/Flanders "Flanders") and [Wallonia](/wiki/Wallonia "Wallonia"), are each subdivided into five [provinces](/wiki/Province "Province"). The third region, [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels"), does not belong to any province, nor is it subdivided into provinces. Instead, it has amalgamated both regional and provincial functions into a single "Capital Region" administration.

Most of the provinces take their name from earlier [duchies](/wiki/Duchy "Duchy") and [counties](/wiki/County "County") of similar location, while their territory is mostly based on the [departments](/wiki/130_departments_of_the_First_French_Empire "130 departments of the First French Empire") installed during French annexation. At the time of the [creation of Belgium](/wiki/Independence_of_Belgium "Independence of Belgium") in 1830, only nine provinces existed, including the [province of Brabant](/wiki/Province_of_Brabant "Province of Brabant"), which held the [City of Brussels](/wiki/City_of_Brussels "City of Brussels"). In 1995, Brabant was split into three areas: [Flemish Brabant](/wiki/Flemish_Brabant "Flemish Brabant"), which became a part of the region of Flanders; [Walloon Brabant](/wiki/Walloon_Brabant "Walloon Brabant"), which became part of the region of Wallonia; and the [Brussels-Capital Region](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels"), which became a third region. These divisions reflected political tensions between the French-speaking Walloons and the Dutch-speaking Flemish; the Brussels-Capital Region is officially bilingual.

The division into provinces is fixed by Article 5 of the [Belgian Constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Belgium "Constitution of Belgium"). The provinces and Brussels are subdivided into 43 administrative [arrondissements](/wiki/Arrondissements_of_Belgium "Arrondissements of Belgium"), and further into 565 [municipalities](/wiki/Municipalities_of_Belgium "Municipalities of Belgium").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Provinces_of_Belgium&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/Map_of_the_Habsburg_Netherlands_by_Alexis-Marie_Gochet.png/250px-Map_of_the_Habsburg_Netherlands_by_Alexis-Marie_Gochet.png)](/wiki/File:Map_of_the_Habsburg_Netherlands_by_Alexis-Marie_Gochet.png)

Map of the Seventeen Provinces, red showing the border between the independent (Northern) [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands") and the [Southern Netherlands](/wiki/Southern_Netherlands "Southern Netherlands").

The medieval [Low Countries](/wiki/Low_Countries "Low Countries"), including present-day Belgium, the Netherlands and Luxembourg, as well as parts of modern Germany and France, comprised a number of rival and independent feudal states of varying sizes. These each had their own identities and governments, though in the early modern period almost all the Belgian states became part of larger entities (the [Seventeen Provinces](/wiki/Seventeen_Provinces "Seventeen Provinces") (1549–1581) and the [Southern Netherlands](/wiki/Southern_Netherlands "Southern Netherlands") (after 1581)). Prominent early states in the area of modern Belgium included the [Duchy of Brabant](/wiki/Duchy_of_Brabant "Duchy of Brabant"), the [County of Flanders](/wiki/County_of_Flanders "County of Flanders"), the [Prince-Bishopric of Liège](/wiki/Prince-Bishopric_of_Li%C3%A8ge "Prince-Bishopric of Liège") and the [Duchy of Luxembourg](/wiki/Duchy_of_Luxembourg "Duchy of Luxembourg"); smaller ones included the [County of Hainaut](/wiki/County_of_Hainaut "County of Hainaut"), the [Duchy of Limburg](/wiki/Duchy_of_Limburg "Duchy of Limburg") and the [County of Namur](/wiki/County_of_Namur "County of Namur"), though there were other small states as well.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/73/France_Departement_1801.svg/250px-France_Departement_1801.svg.png)](/wiki/File:France_Departement_1801.svg)

The [First French Republic](/wiki/First_French_Republic "First French Republic")

When these territories were annexed by France in 1795, they were reorganised into *[départments](/wiki/130_departments_of_the_First_French_Empire "130 departments of the First French Empire")*; the borders were redrawn and the historical names were replaced by names of geographical features (generally the main river of the area).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ce/Belgique_1830.jpg/250px-Belgique_1830.jpg)](/wiki/File:Belgique_1830.jpg)

Belgium at its independence in 1830

At the end of French rule and the creation of the [United Kingdom of the Netherlands](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_the_Netherlands "United Kingdom of the Netherlands") in 1815, the departmental territories were generally retained but were renamed into provinces and the historical names returned. At the time of the [independence of Belgium](/wiki/Independence_of_Belgium "Independence of Belgium") from the Netherlands in 1830, Belgium's territory simply consisted of the existing nine southern provinces. The first article of the [Belgian Constitution](/wiki/Belgian_Constitution "Belgian Constitution") said: "Belgium is divided into provinces. These provinces are Antwerp, Brabant, West Flanders, East Flanders, Hainaut, Liège, Limburg, Luxembourg, Namur, except for the relations of Luxembourg with the [German Confederation](/wiki/German_Confederation "German Confederation")." As such, each of the modern provinces of Belgium (with the exception of [Antwerp](/wiki/Antwerp_Province "Antwerp Province")) takes its name from one of the medieval predecessors, whereas the borders largely correspond to those of the French departments, which in most cases differ substantially from the historical entities.

In 1839, as part of the [Treaty of London](/wiki/Treaty_of_London_(1839) "Treaty of London (1839)"), half of the province of Limburg became part of the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands"), which consequently has its own [province of Limburg](/wiki/Limburg_(Netherlands) "Limburg (Netherlands)").

In 1920, following the [First World War](/wiki/First_World_War "First World War"), Belgium annexed the [Eupen-Malmedy](/wiki/Eupen-Malmedy "Eupen-Malmedy") territory, which became part of [Liège Province](/wiki/Li%C3%A8ge_Province "Liège Province").

During the second half of the 20th century, Belgium transitioned from a unitary state to a federal state with [three Communities and three Regions](/wiki/Communities,_regions_and_language_areas_of_Belgium "Communities, regions and language areas of Belgium"). As part of the [state reforms](/wiki/State_reform_in_Belgium "State reform in Belgium"), the (bilingual) [province of Brabant](/wiki/Province_of_Brabant "Province of Brabant") was split in 1995 three ways: into two (unilingual) provinces (Flemish Brabant and Walloon Brabant) and into the (bilingual) [Brussels-Capital Region](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels"). (The Brussels-Capital Region does not belong to any province, is not a province, and does not contain any provinces.) The two new Brabant provinces became part of the Flemish Region and the Walloon Region respectively. The remaining eight provinces became part of these regions as well, so the Flemish Region and the Walloon Region each contain five provinces.

## Provincial governments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Provinces_of_Belgium&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Provincial governments")]

The provincial government consists of three main branches: the *Provincial Council*, which is the legislative body, the *Deputation* or *Provincial College*, which is the executive body, and the *Governor*, who is appointed by the regional government (i.e. the Flemish or Walloon Government).

The Provincial Councils ([Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language"): *Provincieraad*; [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): *Conseil provincial*) are the representative bodies of the population of the provinces. This is the equivalent of the [States-Provincial](/wiki/States-Provincial "States-Provincial") in the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands"). The numbers of seats in the Provincial Councils are proportional to the population of the province; the numbers were reduced in both Flanders and Wallonia, starting 2013 (following the [2012 elections](/wiki/Belgian_provincial_and_municipal_elections,_2012 "Belgian provincial and municipal elections, 2012")). They are directly elected each six years, at the same time of the municipal elections. Before 1994, the provincial elections instead coincided with the national elections. Until then, the provincial councils also appointed Provincial Senators to the [Belgian Senate](/wiki/Belgian_Senate "Belgian Senate"). The last elections were held on [14 October 2018](/wiki/Belgian_local_elections,_2018 "Belgian local elections, 2018").

The executive branch was previously called the *Permanent Deputation*. In the [Flemish Region](/wiki/Flemish_Region "Flemish Region") it is now simply called the Deputation ([Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language"): *Deputatie*) and it consists of the Governor and six Deputies elected by the Provincial Council from among its members. Following the next 2018 election, there will be one Deputy less, i.e. five Deputies. In the [Walloon Region](/wiki/Walloon_Region "Walloon Region") it is called the Provincial College ([French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): *Collège provincial*) which consists of the Governor and four to five Deputies (depending on the number of inhabitants of the province) elected by the Provincial Council from among its members.

In [Flemish Brabant](/wiki/Flemish_Brabant "Flemish Brabant"), there is also a Deputy Governor ([Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language"): *Adjunct van de gouverneur*). The Deputy Governor is appointed by the [Flemish Government](/wiki/Flemish_Government "Flemish Government") on the unanimous advice of the [Federal Council of Ministers](/wiki/Belgian_Federal_Government "Belgian Federal Government") and must have a considerable knowledge of both the [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language") and the [French language](/wiki/French_language "French language"). He is responsible for ensuring that the language legislation is observed in the municipalities in the [Brussels Periphery](/wiki/Brussels_Periphery "Brussels Periphery").

Following the Fifth [State Reform](/wiki/State_reform_in_Belgium "State reform in Belgium"), the responsibility for the provincial institutions was devolved to the Regions. The Regions have the power to amend or replace the existing legislation on the provincial institutions, most notably the Provincial Law of 30 April 1836. In the Flemish Region, the Provincial Decree of 9 December 2005 applies. In the Walloon Region, the Code of Local Democracy and Decentralisation applies. The legal framework in these Regions is still very similar, but that could change in the future. Although the Regions are responsible for the provincial institutions, the Federal State has retained its responsibility over the provinces in certain cases. For instance, the Regions are responsible for the appointment of the Provincial Governors, but only after the unanimous advice of the [Federal Council of Ministers](/wiki/Belgian_Federal_Government "Belgian Federal Government"). Legislation regarding the Governor and Vice-Governor of [Brussels-Capital](/wiki/Arrondissement_of_Brussels-Capital "Arrondissement of Brussels-Capital"), and the Deputy Governor of [Flemish Brabant](/wiki/Flemish_Brabant "Flemish Brabant"), has also remained a federal competency.

## Absence of any province in the Brussels Capital Region

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Provinces_of_Belgium&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Absence of any province in the Brussels Capital Region")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2e/BelgiumBrussels.png/250px-BelgiumBrussels.png)](/wiki/File:BelgiumBrussels.png)

The [Brussels Capital Region](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels") in [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium").

The [Brussels Capital Region](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels") does not belong to any province, nor does it contain any. The extraprovincial status of Brussels has existed since 1995, when the former [province of Brabant](/wiki/Province_of_Brabant "Province of Brabant"), which had Brussels as its capital, was divided into the Dutch-speaking province of [Flemish Brabant](/wiki/Flemish_Brabant "Flemish Brabant") and the French-speaking province of [Walloon Brabant](/wiki/Walloon_Brabant "Walloon Brabant").

Within this mainly French speaking region, nearly all former provincial competencies are assumed by its regional institutions and by the [French Community Commission](/wiki/French_Community_Commission "French Community Commission"), the [Flemish Community Commission](/wiki/Flemish_Community_Commission "Flemish Community Commission") or the [Common Community Commission](/wiki/Common_Community_Commission "Common Community Commission"). However, the [Arrondissement of Brussels-Capital](/wiki/Arrondissement_of_Brussels-Capital "Arrondissement of Brussels-Capital") has two commissioners of the [Federal Government](/wiki/Belgian_Federal_Government "Belgian Federal Government") who are called "[Governor of the Brussels-Capital Region](/wiki/Governor_of_the_Brussels-Capital_Region "Governor of the Brussels-Capital Region")" and "Vice-Governor". The Governor exercises most of the few remaining powers elsewhere exercised by a provincial governor, particularly in the field of public order, as far as no (federal) law, (regional) decree, ordinance or decision states otherwise.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

The Governor is appointed by the [cabinet of the Brussels Capital Region](/wiki/Politics_and_government_of_the_Brussels-Capital_Region#Cabinet "Politics and government of the Brussels-Capital Region") on the unanimous advice of the Federal Council of Ministers. The regional government also appoints the Vice-Governor, who must have a considerable knowledge of both [French](/wiki/French_language "French language") and [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language") and who must ensure that the legislation regarding the use of the two languages is observed in Brussels.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## Proposed additional province

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Provinces_of_Belgium&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Proposed additional province")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4f/Duitstalige_GemeenschapLocatie.png/250px-Duitstalige_GemeenschapLocatie.png)](/wiki/File:Duitstalige_GemeenschapLocatie.png)

The German-speaking Community.

Because the [German-speaking Community](/wiki/German-speaking_Community_of_Belgium "German-speaking Community of Belgium") is located entirely within [Liège Province](/wiki/Li%C3%A8ge_Province "Liège Province"), it has been proposed on multiple occasions to create an eleventh province, the province of [Eupen](/wiki/Eupen "Eupen")-[Sankt Vith](/wiki/St._Vith "St. Vith"), which would comprise the nine municipalities of the German-speaking Community. Most of the functions carried out by provincial organs would then be exercised by the organs of the German-speaking Community.[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7)

However, the community is small in area (854 km2 or 330 sq mi) and has only about 76,000 inhabitants, which would make it the smallest and by far the least populated province.
